findings.message: D6 hard-codes the Phase 2 auto-render default to `--cluster-depth 2`, but the same ADR later says the Phase 2 default is still an open question between `1` and `2`. That internal contradiction leaves the implementation target ambiguous.

`spec.json` is marking inferred requirements as blue-sourced facts. The TDD item cites `.claude/rules/05-testing.md §Core Principles`, but that source only requires TDD in general and does not justify the specific `empty graph / single edge / multi-edge / enum shape` test matrix; likewise the CI acceptance text cites `§Pre-commit Checklist` for a narrower `cargo make ci` breakdown it does not define. Under `knowledge/conventions/source-attribution.md`, these items should use inference/discussion sources or cite a document that actually states them.

T002 does not include the required wiring in `apps/cli/src/commands/track/mod.rs`, which is where `TrackCommand` and `execute()` dispatch are defined today. Following this scope literally would only add `graph.rs` and `tddd/mod.rs`, leaving `sotp track type-graph` unreachable and failing the Phase 1 acceptance criterion.

The T003 acceptance criterion still describes `cargo make ci` as only `fmt-check + clippy -D warnings + nextest + deny + check-layers`, but the current Makefile defines `ci` as the full `ci-local` suite and even `ci-rust` also runs `verify-canonical-modules-local`. This under-specifies the real gate, so the plan can mark verification complete while the actual CI command still fails.

T002 still describes CLI dispatch as a single ambiguous `mod.rs` registration here, while `spec.json` now correctly requires wiring both `apps/cli/src/commands/track/tddd/mod.rs` and the top-level `apps/cli/src/commands/track/mod.rs`. Because `plan.md` is generated from this metadata, an implementer can still miss the `TrackCommand`/`execute()` wiring and end up with an unreachable `sotp track type-graph` command.

The plan/spec still describe trait-impl edges as deferred because of a future `tddd-05` dependency, but `TypeNode::trait_impls` is already present in `libs/domain/src/schema.rs`. That makes the out-of-scope note factually wrong and can mislead Phase 2 planning by treating an already-satisfied prerequisite as a blocker.

`updated_at` is earlier than `created_at` (`2026-04-16T12:47:17Z` vs `2026-04-16T22:30:00Z`), so this record claims it was last modified before it was created. Any sync or freshness logic that trusts these timestamps will misclassify the item.

`updated_at` was left at the track creation timestamp even though this transition changes `metadata.json` from `planned` to `in_progress` and marks T001 done. The track workflow uses `updated_at` as the fallback for resolving the latest active track on non-`track/*` branches, so leaving it stale can misorder or skip this track in later commands. Bump it to the actual transition time when recording the T001 completion.

`metadata.json` records the T001 transition to `done`, but `updated_at` is still the original creation timestamp (`2026-04-16T22:30:00Z`). Track mutations are expected to refresh `updated_at`, and downstream workflow/docs use this field to identify the latest track state; leaving it stale makes this transition factually inconsistent and can cause freshness/order bugs.

`metadata.json` marks T001 as `done` and moves the track to `in_progress`, but `updated_at` is still the original creation timestamp. Latest-track resolution and freshness checks rank tracks by `metadata.json.updated_at`, so leaving it stale after this transition can misorder the track or make downstream tooling read an older state than the file now represents.

`updated_at` was changed to `2026-04-16T12:47:05Z`, but `created_at` is still `2026-04-16T22:30:00Z`, so the item now appears updated before it was created. This reintroduces a factual contradiction in the metadata and can break any ordering logic that assumes `updated_at >= created_at`.

`verification.md` records T003 as fully completed (`cargo make ci` passed, readability confirmed, and the result table marks `T003 | Done`), but `metadata.json` and the generated `plan.md` still leave T003 as `todo`. These scoped artifacts now disagree on whether the CI/readability work actually happened, which violates the briefing's no-contradictions requirement and can cause downstream tooling or reviewers to treat the track as fully verified when it is still in progress.

`updated_at` is still `2026-04-16T22:47:05Z` even though this patch also marks `T003` and the overall track `done`, and `verification.md` records that final completed state later at `2026-04-16T23:30:00Z`. On `main`, latest-track resolution falls back to `updated_at`, so leaving the timestamp stale can misorder track selection and misrepresent which track was updated most recently.

The new `execute_type_graph` entry point only has negative-case tests. There is no coverage for the success path or the actual write path (`RustdocSchemaExporter` -> `build_type_graph` -> `render_type_graph_flat` -> `atomic_write_file`), so a regression in the newly added `type-graph` dispatch or `<layer>-graph.md` output would not be caught.

`execute_type_graph` writes `<layer>-graph.md` through `atomic_write_file` without the symlink guards that `baseline-capture` uses. A symlinked `items_dir` or `track/<id>` can redirect the write outside the track tree and overwrite an arbitrary file, so this new command is vulnerable to path-traversal-by-symlink.

The new public `execute_type_graph` API has no happy-path test. The current tests only cover invalid track IDs, unknown layers, and frozen tracks, so regressions in the actual rustdoc export/render/write flow will not be caught.

`execute_type_graph` only checks whether `items_dir` itself is a symlink, then reads `items_dir/<track_id>/metadata.json` directly. A symlinked track directory under a real items root will still be followed here, so the command can load metadata from outside the trusted tree before the active-track guard runs. The later output-path guard in `render_and_write_graph` does not protect this read path.

This only rejects a symlink on the final `items_dir` component. If `--items-dir` is reached through a symlinked ancestor (for example `repo-link/track/items`), `reject_symlinks_below()` will still trust that anchor and the metadata read / atomic write can escape the intended workspace. Canonicalize or validate the full anchor path before treating `items_dir` as trusted.

`extract_type_names` is too permissive: it treats every PascalCase token in the rendered return string as a target, so signatures like `impl Display`/`dyn Debug` and wrapper-heavy returns like `Result<Option<User>, DomainError>` can produce false edges to `Display`, `Debug`, `Result`, or `Option` whenever those names exist in the graph. That makes the diagram report dependencies that do not exist; reuse the structured return-type extraction logic instead of token-splitting the string.

`extract_type_names` is still too coarse for common Rust signatures: it will surface associated-type labels like `Item` and `Output` from returns such as `impl Iterator<Item = Foo>` / `impl Future<Output = Foo>`, and the new whitelist still misses other standard wrappers/traits like `IntoIterator`, `Clone`, `Copy`, and `Default`. If any of those names exist in the `TypeGraph`, the renderer will emit false edges.

The new assoc-type label filter is too broad: once a label name like `Item`/`Output` is collected, every matching token is dropped from the return string. That silently removes valid edges when a real type shares the same name, e.g. `impl Iterator<Item = Item>` or `impl Future<Output = Output>`.

`STDLIB_WRAPPER_TYPES` still omits common standard-library trait tokens such as `TryFrom` and `TryInto`. `extract_type_names()` will emit those names for signatures like `impl TryFrom<Foo>`, so if the graph ever contains a same-named node the renderer will still create a false edge, leaving this class of dependency-noise unresolved.

`STDLIB_WRAPPER_TYPES` is still incomplete: common std wrappers such as `PhantomData` (and `Cow`) are not filtered, so a method returning `PhantomData<Foo>` can still produce a false edge whenever a `PhantomData` node exists in the graph. That defeats the new guard against wrapper-like return tokens becoming diagram edges.

`STDLIB_WRAPPER_TYPES` lists only the bare `NonZero` token here, but `extract_type_names()` emits concrete identifiers like `NonZeroU32` and `NonZeroI64` as single tokens. Those common stdlib wrappers will still pass the filter and can create false edges whenever a graph contains a same-named type.

The stdlib-wrapper denylist is still incomplete for common single-token wrappers. Types like `Poll`, `ControlFlow`, and `Range*` are not filtered here, so a method returning `Poll<Published>` or `Range<User>` will still emit false edges whenever the graph contains those node names. That leaves the same false-positive bug this patch is meant to remove.

`extract_type_names` tokenizes return types only by separators, so common associated-type bindings like `impl Iterator<Item = Foo>` or `impl Future<Output = Bar>` will surface `Item`/`Output` as candidate types. If the workspace has a type with one of those names, the renderer adds a bogus edge; this needs label-aware parsing or reuse of the structured type-name extraction instead of raw token splitting.

`render_type_graph_flat` documents `EdgeSet::Fields`, `EdgeSet::Impls`, and `EdgeSet::All` as supported modes, but the implementation only ever collects method edges. A caller that selects `Fields` or `Impls` gets an empty diagram, and `All` still omits those edge classes entirely, so the public API cannot satisfy its own contract.

The `max_nodes_per_diagram` truncation slices the alphabetically sorted connected-node set before filtering edges, so a small limit can drop every rendered edge and leave a diagram of isolated nodes even when the graph has relationships. For example, with edges `A -> Z` and `B -> C`, a limit of 2 keeps `A` and `B` but renders no edges at all. The truncation step needs to preserve an actual edge-induced subgraph, not an arbitrary node prefix.

`layer_id` is interpolated directly into `graph_filename`, and the symlink guard does not normalize `..` segments. A caller that passes an unvalidated id like `../../escape` can make `graph_path` escape `track_dir` and write outside `trusted_root`. This helper needs its own filename validation or a validated type boundary.

This new public API has no tests covering the successful write path or the symlink-rejection/error path. That leaves the `reject_symlinks_below` + `atomic_write_file` integration unverified and violates the repo's public-API testing requirement.

This test is unconditional but uses `std::os::unix::fs::symlink`, so `cargo test` will fail to compile on non-Unix targets. Gate the test with `#[cfg(unix)]` (or provide a cross-platform fallback) to keep the infrastructure crate buildable everywhere.

This `layer_id` check is stricter than the existing TDDD layer-id contract. `parse_tddd_layers` only rejects path separators and `..`, but this code now rejects any id containing `.` or non-ASCII characters, so a configuration that the infrastructure already accepts can start failing here even though the filename would still be safe. Align the validation with the shared path-component rules or move to a validated `LayerId` type.

P0: The new `layer_id` validation still allows a single `:` character, so on Windows inputs like `C:escape` pass the check and `track_dir.join(format!("{layer_id}-graph.md"))` becomes a drive-prefixed path instead of a child of `track_dir`. That lets `write_type_graph_file` escape the intended output directory despite the traversal guard added here.
